title: Floating-Point Numbers
summary: Represent real numbers using IEEE 754 floating-point format, and understand precision limits.
content_html:
Floating-point representation stores real numbers (decimals) in a form similar to scientific notation: sign × mantissa × 2^exponent.

IEEE 754 Single Precision (32-bit)

  • 1 bit: sign (0=positive, 1=negative)
  • 8 bits: exponent (biased by 127)
  • 23 bits: mantissa (fractional part, 1.xxx implied)
Value = (−1)^sign × 1.mantissa × 2^(exponent − 127)

Example: 78.0 in IEEE 754

78 = 1.21875 × 2^6 = 1.001110₂ × 2^6
Sign = 0
Exponent = 6 + 127 = 133 = 10000101₂
Mantissa = 00111000000000000000000
Result: 0 10000101 00111000000000000000000

Special Values

  • +Infinity: exponent all 1s, mantissa 0
  • NaN (Not a Number): exponent all 1s, mantissa non-zero
  • Zero: all bits 0

Key Limitation

Floating-point numbers are approximations. Not all decimals can be represented exactly (e.g., 0.1 in binary is repeating). This causes rounding errors in calculations.

Analogue vs Digital

The real world is analogue (continuous). Converting to digital (sampling) introduces quantisation error — more bits = more precision.

exercises.title: NaN
exercises.prompt: In IEEE 754, NaN (Not a Number) occurs when:
options: Exponent is all 1s and mantissa is non-zero
exercises.answer: Exponent is all 1s and mantissa is non-zero

exercises.title: Floating-point limitation
exercises.prompt: Why can't all decimal numbers be stored exactly in floating-point?
options: Many decimals like 0.1 are repeating in binary
exercises.answer: Many decimals like 0.1 are repeating in binary

options: Sampling discretises a continuous value, causing quantisation error
